chunk_summary: This section outlines age-specific feeding skills and texture milestones to address feeding difficulties and faltering growth, emphasizing developmental alignment and practical strategies for Indian clinical settings.
learning_objectives: Understand age-appropriate feeding skills and texture progression from 6 months to 5 years.
Identify and address common feeding difficulties such as delayed texture progression and milk displacement.
Apply structured meal and snack routines to prevent feeding aversion and grazing behaviors.
Recognize when to refer for feeding therapy in cases of missed milestones or persistent feeding issues.
key_facts: At 6–8 months, introduce thick, energy-dense complementary foods with 2–3 meals/day and use the 'spoon stays coated' rule to avoid watery feeds.
By 9–11 months, progress to mashed, lumpy, and soft finger foods with 3–4 meals/day and 1–2 snacks to encourage self-feeding.
From 12–23 months, transition to family foods with 3–4 meals/day, 1–2 snacks, and limit milk intake to 500–600 mL/day to prevent milk displacement.
For ages 2–5 years, maintain structured meals and snacks, avoid grazing, and use neutral exposure to new foods instead of pressure.
Missed milestones, such as no texture progression or meals lasting >30 minutes, may indicate feeding-skill or psychosocial issues requiring early feeding therapy.
reference_quote: Ages 6–8 months: start thick, energy-dense complementary foods at 6 completed months while continuing breastfeeding; aim for 2–3 meals/day, gradually increasing portions, and teach the 'spoon stays coated' rule to avoid calorie-poor watery feeds. (Source: WHO, Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Preventing faltering growth through appropriate feeding practices
Guidelines for milk intake and texture progression
